  "title": "Extraction Failed",
  "extracted_text": "Extract from: *Fahrenheit 451* by Ray Bradbury (Ballantine Books, USA, 1953)\n\nIt was a pleasure to burn.\n\nIt was a special pleasure to see things eaten, to see things blackened and\nchanged. With the brass nozzle in his fists, with this great python spitting its\nvenomous kerosene upon the world, the blood pounded in his head, and his hands\nwere the hands of some amazing conductor playing all the symphonies of blazing\nand burning to bring down the tatters and charcoal ruins of history. With his symbolic\nhelmet numbered 451 on his stolid head, and his eyes all orange flame with the\nthought of what came next, he flicked the igniter and the house jumped up in a\ngorging fire that burned the evening sky red and yellow and black. He strode in a\nswarm of fireflies. He wanted above all, like the old joke, to shove a marshmallow on\na stick in a furnace, while the flapping pigeon-winged books died on the porch and\nlawn of the house. While the books went up in sparkling whirls and blew away on a\nwind turned dark with burning.\n\nMontag grinned the fierce grin of all men singed and driven back by flame.\n\nHe knew that when he returned to the firehouse, he might wink at himself, a\nminstrel man, burnt-corked, in the mirror. Later, going to sleep, he would feel the\nfiery smile still gripped by his face muscles, in the dark. It never went away, that\nsmile, it never ever went away, as long as he remembered.",

With the brass nozzle in his fists, with this great python spitting its\nvenomous kerosene upon the world, the blood pounded in his head, and his hands\nwere the hands of some amazing conductor playing all the symphonies of blazing\nand burning to bring down the tatters and charcoal ruins of history. With his symbolic\nhelmet numbered 451 on his stolid head, and his eyes all orange flame with the\nthought of what came next, he flicked the igniter and the house jumped up in a\ngorging fire that burned the evening sky red and yellow and black. He strode in a\nswarm of fireflies. He wanted above all, like the old joke, to shove a marshmallow on\na stick in a furnace, while the flapping pigeon-winged books died on the porch and\nlawn of the house. While the books went up in sparkling whirls and blew away on a\nwind turned dark with burning.\n\nMontag grinned the fierce grin of all men singed and driven back by flame.\n\nHe knew that when he returned to the firehouse, he might wink at himself, a\nminstrel man, burnt-corked, in the mirror.
